what_it_does: Implements the Segmented control as a function component using React.createElement (aliased as h). Renders a div with role='tablist' and class cv-segmented, mapping each option to a button with role='tab', type='button', aria-selected, and class cv-segmented__opt. Options may be strings or {value,label} objects; the active option is the one whose id matches value, defaulting to the first option (index 0) when value is null.
observations: Uses global React (/* global React */) and const h = React.createElement instead of JSX
Derives id and label from each option: object → op.value (or op.label as fallback) / op.label; string → the string itself
active = value != null ? value === id : i === 0 — first option is active when value is unset
onClick is only attached when onChange is provided (conditional handler)
className composed as ['cv-segmented', className].filter(Boolean).join(' ') to merge a caller-supplied class
Spreads ...rest onto the wrapper div via Object.assign
standouts: All visual behaviour is delegated to token CSS classes (.cv-segmented, .cv-segmented__opt) — the component carries no inline styles or literals
id falls back to label when an object option omits value, so key/value can silently become the label text
conventions: Follows the design-system rule that components are thin token-class wrappers carrying no raw values (the 'one home' / no-second-place directive)
concerns: When an object option has no value, id and label collapse to the same string; two options sharing a label would collide on key and on active-matching
notes: A token-class-only segmented control; active state is value-controlled with first-option fallback, and clicks emit (id, event) only if onChange is given.
questions: Is the value-defaults-to-first-option behaviour intended for uncontrolled use, or should callers always pass value?
purpose_vs_actual: Match — implements the audience toggle the header comment and .d.ts describe.
intention_signals: To express a segmented audience toggle purely as a structural + ARIA shell over design-system CSS classes, so the look lives entirely in tokens and the JS only handles option normalization and selection.
novelty: Uses React.createElement directly (no JSX/build step) while still presenting a typed component — a deliberately buildless authoring style.
contribution: Contributes the structural/ARIA implementation of the segmented audience toggle, including string-or-object option normalization and first-option-default active logic, all delegated to .cv-segmented token classes.
summary_for_embedding: React.createElement implementation of a Segmented tablist control rendering token-class buttons, with string-or-object options, value-controlled active state defaulting to the first option.
intention_for_embedding: To provide the buildless, token-class-only segmented audience toggle whose visual behaviour lives entirely in design-system CSS.
